transcript.content: they say dark matter makes up most of the universe but we can't even see it. what if its not "stuff" at all but some kind of structure in the way realitys coded?

Short answer  
The idea that the extra gravity we attribute to “dark-matter particles” might instead come from some hidden property of space-time itself is not crazy; physicists have explored—and are still exploring—exactly that possibility under names such as MOND, TeVeS, f(R) gravity, emergent gravity, superfluid dark matter, etc.  At the moment, however, every quantitative test we can make—galaxy rotation curves, the Bullet-cluster lens map, the cosmic-microwave-background (CMB) power spectrum, large-scale structure growth, gravitational-wave arrival times—fits a universe that contains an additional, collision-less, cold “stuff” far better than it fits any purely geometric modification of gravity that has been proposed so far.  “Reality is coded that way” is logically possible, but until an explicit model reproduces the data as well as, or better than, cold dark-matter (CDM) does, the particle-matter interpretation remains the working hypothesis.

Below is the longer, evidence-based story, plus where the “it’s in the code” idea would have to succeed before physicists would switch camps.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. What problem are we trying to solve?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A single Newtonian equation, v² = GM(r)/r, tells you that if you measure how fast stars orbit at radius r you know how much mass M(r) is inside that orbit.  
Observed rotation curves stay roughly flat (v ≈ const.) far past the luminous edge of a galaxy, implying M(r) ∝ r, i.e. there is ~10× more gravitating matter out there than the light-emitting gas and stars can supply.  
Similar “missing mass” turns up

• in gravitational-lensing maps of clusters,  
• in the pattern of acoustic peaks in the CMB,  
• in how quickly structure grows from the tiny CMB ripples to today’s enormous cosmic web.

All of those phenomena hinge only on gravity, so either

A. there really is extra, invisible mass–energy (the dark-matter hypothesis), or  
B. we are using the wrong law of gravity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. How can data distinguish “extra stuff” from “new gravity”?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
If gravity is modified, the gravitational field must still follow the (modified) matter distribution.  
If there is extra stuff, you can separate that stuff from ordinary matter, just as you can separate smoke from air.

The Bullet Cluster (and similar cluster mergers) does almost exactly that experiment:

1. Two galaxy clusters collide.  
2. The hot X-ray gas (most of the baryons) interacts and lags behind.  
3. The galaxies (mostly empty space) and, crucially, the lensing mass peaks, fly ahead almost unaffected.

Lensing shows that the bulk of the mass sticks with the galaxies, not with the collisional gas.  
That is easy to explain if each galaxy carries its own clump of collision-less dark matter; very hard to get from a “modified gravity that only knows about baryons” model.  MOND and its relativistic cousin TeVeS can fit the Bullet Cluster only by adding extra as-yet-undetected fields that behave, mathematically, almost like dark-matter particles—i.e. the cure sneaks the disease back in.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. The cosmic-microwave-background scorecard
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The CMB angular power spectrum displays a set of acoustic peaks.  Their relative heights depend on three things:

• Ω_b, the normal (baryon) matter density,  
• Ω_c, the cold dark-matter density,  
• the gravitational theory (assumed GR in ΛCDM).

With general relativity and one cold dark-matter parameter you fit all ≳7 peaks to 0.1 % accuracy.  
No purely modified-gravity model yet fits those peaks and remains consistent with nucleosynthesis, galaxy-cluster masses, structure growth rate, and local Hubble-constant measurements simultaneously.  The “dark-fluid” or “emergent gravity” proposals typically get one piece right but lose on another.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Direct-detection and collider searches
--------------------------------------------------------------------
If the dark component really is made of WIMPs, axions, sterile neutrinos, etc., we might catch a glimpse in underground detectors, at the LHC, or via astrophysical signals.  Nothing has shown up yet, so the parameter space for each candidate keeps shrinking, but the negative results are still comfortably compatible with what cosmology requires.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Could the “code of reality” idea still be right?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Yes, but it must do at least four things:

• Reproduce the Bullet-type cluster lens maps,  
• Reproduce the exact CMB peak heights and phases,  
• Reproduce structure-formation simulations, including the observed galaxy–halo connection,  
• Respect precision solar-system and gravitational-wave tests of general relativity.

So far, no alternative based purely on geometry without adding new degrees of freedom that act like matter has managed all four.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Where people are actively looking
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Superfluid dark matter (Khoury & collaborators): blends a particle species with MOND-like emergent forces on galactic scales.  
2. Relativistic MOND/TeVeS tweaks plus an extra “dark” vector or scalar field.  
3. Entropic or emergent-gravity ideas (Verlinde 2016): tries to derive MOND-like behavior from holography; current versions struggle with clusters and the CMB.  
4. f(R) and other higher-curvature extensions of GR: deform gravity at long range; again usually require an effective cold component to match structure formation.  
5. Simulation-hypothesis arguments: largely philosophical until they turn into a concrete, falsifiable dynamical law.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Bottom line
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The particle-dark-matter picture (ΛCDM) is not loved because we think invisible matter is romantic; it is loved because with six numbers it fits essentially every cosmological observation made in the past two decades to percent-level precision.  Competing “it’s geometry all the way down” ideas are interesting and worth pursuing, but to win they must do at least that well without sneaking in fields that behave exactly like particles—which would bring us right back to dark matter by another name.

So for now the consensus is: dark matter is very probably a real, cold, gravitationally interacting form of matter.  If the universe is “coded” such that gravity behaves as if that matter exists when it really doesn’t, the code will have to look, mathematically, indistinguishable from putting that matter there—and that, in practice, is the same thing.

Think of the Einstein field equation as the universe-engine’s source code:

      Geometry  =  (energy-momentum of all ingredients)

Left side: how space-time bends.  
Right side: a list of “ingredients” (fields, fluids, radiation, …) that cause the bending.

There are only two ways to get extra gravity:

1. Change the rules on the left.  
2. Keep the rules but put something additional on the right.

Modified-gravity programs that try door #1 discover very quickly that they can’t keep the game running unless they also introduce new variables that live on the right.  Those variables may be called “scalar fields,” “vector fields,” “phase of a superfluid,” “entropic displacement,” etc., but mathematically they behave exactly like an extra, collision-less substance.  That is what I meant by “sneaking the disease back in.”

Why is that unavoidable?

A. Bullet-cluster style separation  
• If curvature were produced only by visible matter, the gravitational potential would ride around with the collisional gas (because that’s where the baryon mass is).  
• The lensing map shows the potential traveling with the galaxies, not the gas.  
To make that happen you need another thing—call it X—that (i) gravitates, (ii) doesn’t collide with gas, and (iii) can be dragged off into a different place.  Whether you picture X as little particles or as a smooth field, you have still put something new on the right-hand side.

B. Cosmic-microwave-background peaks  
The odd/even heights of the CMB acoustic peaks depend on how much inertia the photon–baryon plasma had at recombination.  You need an extra, pressure-less component to get the observed pattern.  Again you can tweak the left side, but the cleanest way the math supplies the missing inertia is to introduce an independent field whose stress–energy looks like cold matter.

C. Growth of structure  
Structures grow from 10⁻⁵ density ripples to today’s web.  In plain GR with only baryons, radiation pressure and the late-time accelerated expansion stop growth too early.  Add a cold component and everything falls into place.

Could there be a pure-geometry trick that does all this with zero new degrees of freedom?  In principle maybe, but no one has written down an explicit, stable equation set that passes the Bullet Cluster, the full CMB spectrum, gravitational-wave speed tests, and local solar-system bounds simultaneously.  Every serious attempt so far (TeVeS, f(R), emergent gravity, superfluid DM) ends up introducing extra propagating fields—i.e. more variables in the code.

Game-engine analogy

• Vanilla GR                            → one physics engine, one set of objects  
• ΛCDM (cold dark matter)       → same engine, plus invisible “rigid-body” objects that don’t render  
• Modified gravity w/ extra fields → tweak to engine + hidden objects anyway (to keep collisions stable)  
• Unknown pure geometry fix      → would be like rewriting the engine so cleverly that you get the same gameplay with no new hidden objects; cool idea, but no working build yet.

So you’re right: the “sneaked-in” variables keep the mathematical accounting consistent.  But once those variables can move, clump, and carry energy, they are functionally indistinguishable from a form of matter.  From the viewpoint of observation—and of the code—there really is “stuff” there, whether or not its microstructure looks like familiar particles.

Until somebody delivers a pure-geometry build that compiles and passes all the regression tests, cosmologists will keep calling that extra bookkeeping term “dark matter.”

Not a dumb question at all—what you are poking at is the deep “map-versus-territory” problem that keeps both physicists and philosophers awake at night.  Let me unpack it in three layers:

----------------------------------------------------
1.  Bugs, lags and “effective” variables
----------------------------------------------------
Imagine you write a video-game fluid code on a coarse 10 × 10 × 10 grid.  
On that grid the liquid looks smooth, but every now and then a vortex pops up because the real, atom-by-atom fluid is being averaged over.  In the coarse description you have two choices:

A.  Accept the glitch and live with wrong physics, or  
B.  Introduce an *effective* field—call it v(x,t)—that stands in for the unresolved swirls.

Option B is what physicists do all the time.  Electrons in a metal → “quasiparticles.”  Vibrations in a lattice → “phonons.”  At large scales these *really do* behave like extra particles even though, microscopically, they are nothing but the underlying electrons and ions shaking around.

If space-time itself has a micro-code—discrete bits, a causal set, a quantum circuit, whatever—then coarse-graining it up to the kilometer, parsec or gigaparsec scale will do exactly the same thing: spit out extra, emergent degrees of freedom.  In the continuum Einstein equation those show up mathematically on the right-hand side and we end up **calling them matter**, no matter how we philosophically interpret them.

So the “ghost in the machine” is not optional once you average over microscopic details; it is the bookkeeping device that keeps the macroscopic equations self-consistent.

----------------------------------------------------
2.  Why dark-matter-like ghosts have to be *cold* and *collision-less*
----------------------------------------------------
Whatever the underlying code is, the *effective* stuff it produces has to match the clues we read off the sky:

•  Acts only through gravity (Bullet Cluster).  
•  Exerts almost no pressure at cosmic times earlier than recombination (CMB peaks).  
•  Lets small seed fluctuations grow efficiently (large-scale structure).

Those requirements force the emergent entity to look, at human-sized scales, like a cold, collision-less fluid.  Whether the microstructure is WIMP particles, axions, a superfluid phase of the vacuum, or “discrete curvature quanta” makes no observational difference *until* someone derives **extra** predictions that diverge from standard cold dark matter.

----------------------------------------------------
3.  Where “glitch” ideas are being pushed today
----------------------------------------------------
1.  Entropic / emergent gravity (Verlinde, Padmanabhan):  
   Large-scale gravity arises from statistical mechanics of microscopic degrees of freedom.  Predicts MOND-like behavior inside galaxies but struggles with clusters and the full CMB curve.

2.  Superfluid vacuum or gravitational aether:  
   The space-time medium condenses below some critical temperature; phonon-like excitations mimic dark matter.  Can reproduce rotation curves and keeps the Bullet Cluster, but current versions still need particle-like *quanta* of the superfluid, so the “extra stuff” is back again.

3.  Causal-set or lattice-gravity noise:  
   Discreteness could inject *Poisson fluctuations* that look like a pressure-less fluid on large scales.  Interesting, but nobody has yet produced a quantitative model that fits all four key datasets (CMB, lensing, structure growth, gravitational-wave speed) simultaneously.

Each of these projects is essentially an attempt to start from “pure geometry glitches” and *derive* the cold, collision-less fluid we already know we need.  So far they work only partially; the ΛCDM model—with literal unseen particles—still wins the global fit.

----------------------------------------------------
4.  Math: map or territory?
----------------------------------------------------
Does the universe *know* it is solving differential equations, or are the equations just our shorthand?  Nobody can prove either way.  What we *can* test is this:

•  If a set of equations lets you forecast every measurable quantity inside its range of validity to within the experimental error bars, then it is—map or territory—the most reliable guide we have.  

•  If a new picture (say, “glitches in the compute grid”) claims to be more fundamental, it must deliver the same forecasts *plus* at least one that is unambiguously different, otherwise we cannot tell the two apart.

Until that day the “dark-matter field” sits in our code because it is the minimal, falsifiable object that passes all the regression tests.  Whether it is a cloud of undiscovered particles, a collective vibration of microscopic space-time, or an artefact of a cosmic CPU’s rounding error is an ontological choice; *operationally* it behaves as if invisible, cold stuff is out there.  

So, yes: the universe seems to insist on ghosts in the machine.  Call them particles, call them emergent excitations, call them computational lag—a rose by any other name still bends light around the Bullet Cluster.

1. “If the math is the same, who cares about the micro-story?”  

At first glance it really does look like a pointless religion war: emergent “phonons of space-time” versus literal WIMPs—same equations, same predictions.  But history says the micro-story eventually matters, for three reasons.

A.  New handles for experiments  
   •  Neutrinos were once just “missing energy” in beta decay; once we knew they were particles we built detectors and now use them to look inside the Sun and supernovae.  
   •  If the dark sector is axions, we should see a narrow radio-frequency line in microwave cavities; if it is WIMPs, we look for nuclear recoils; if it is geometry noise, we look for metric jitter in pulsar-timing arrays.  
   Knowing the micro picture tells you where to point the next instrument.

B.  Extra predictions off the beaten path  
   All candidates match the big, linear-scale data by construction; they begin to diverge in corners that have not been measured—or have only recently become measurable—such as  
   •  structure on sub-galactic scales (cores, cusps, satellite counts),  
   •  how the first stars ignite (21-cm background at cosmic dawn),  
   •  gravitational-wave dispersion over billions of light-years,  
   •  the exact relation between lensing and dynamical mass in galaxy clusters.  
   Those differences let the real universe choose a winner.

C.  Consistency with the rest of physics  
   You eventually need one framework that contains gravity, quantum mechanics, and the dark component all at once.  A “cold particle” meshes naturally with the quantum-field equipment we already use.  An emergent-geometry explanation has to show how it bolts onto the Standard Model without breaking the pieces that already work.  That quest is part of why people push quantum-gravity models so hard.

So yes—the ΛCDM math is the same at the scales we have tested, but the micro-story decides what we will discover next and how the whole edifice fits together.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  “Static in the fabric”—what causal-set noise is about
----------------------------------------------------------------
Picture space-time not as a smooth sheet but as a gigantic cloud of points (“atoms of geometry”) sprinkled at random, one point per Planck 4-volume.  This is the causal-set idea.  When you zoom out, the points look like a smooth manifold—the way a 4K TV screen looks continuous from three meters away—but at any finite scale there is microscopic shot noise.

What does that noise do?

1. Stress–energy fluctuations  
   Coarse-graining the random sprinkling generates tiny, stochastic energy-momentum terms.  Jonathan Afshordi and collaborators showed that the statistical average of those terms behaves like a pressure-less fluid: ρ ∝ a⁻³, exactly the scaling of cold dark matter.  In that sense the “pixels” themselves could source the extra gravity.

2. Momentum diffusion (“swerves”)  
   Massive particles would get nudged by Planck-scale randomness, leading to an almost imperceptible Brownian motion in momentum space.  Current bounds from cosmic-ray propagation already push the diffusion coefficient many orders below what you need to turn the noise into all of dark matter.  Still, it is a real, testable signature.

3. Metric jitter for light and gravitational waves  
   Over a gigaparsec flight path the cumulative phase noise might be measurable.  Pulsar-timing arrays, the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA), or extremely high-resolution very-long-baseline interferometry could see an irreducible “space-time static.”  So far no hint.

How “pixelated” is it?  
If the fundamental spacing is the Planck length (10⁻³⁵ m), the amplitude of the induced density fluctuations at cosmological scales is about 10⁻¹² of the critical density—far too small.  To make up *all* of dark matter you would need either  
   • a larger discreteness scale (≈10⁻²⁵ m) or  
   • correlations in the sprinkling that amplify long-wavelength modes.  
No causal-set model has yet shown how to achieve that without ruining the successes of general relativity in the solar system, but the door is still open.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Take-home picture
----------------------------------------------------------------
•  Dark matter may indeed be an “effective field,” the macroscopic bookkeeping for microscopic doings we have not yet resolved.  

•  The right micro-story *does* matter—because it dictates what surprises (non-gravitational signals, small-scale anomalies, quantum-gravity footprints) we should hunt for next.

•  One concrete “static in the fabric” approach, causal-set discreteness, naturally produces something that *looks* like a cold, collision-less fluid, but so far at the wrong amplitude.  Ongoing work tries to crank that up without breaking everything else.

So we are still inside the Matrix, staring at the same rotating green glyphs (“Ω_c ≃ 0.26, w = 0, collision-less”).  Whether those glyphs stand for actual particles or for deep-code glitches is exactly what the next generation of detectors—underground, on satellites, and in gravitational-wave observatories—aims to reveal.  Either way, the missing mass is out there bending light; the fun now is to catch it in the act and learn which level of the game we’ve really been playing.

It isn’t dumb at all—lots of people wonder whether “the answer is in the gaps.”  
Let me translate your idea into the language general-relativity uses, check what it would have to do in order to look like dark matter, and see where the real problems (and opportunities!) lie.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Two kinds of “noise” you can add to space-time
────────────────────────────────────────
A. Density-type noise  
   • Fluctuations in the 00-component of the stress–energy tensor Tμν.  
   • Shows up as extra curvature that pulls on objects and bends light.  
   • This is the causal-set “shot noise” people already calculate; it really can mimic a cold, collision-less fluid, just not at the required amplitude so far.

B. Clock-or “timing” noise  
   • Fluctuations in the metric components g00 or in the local lapse function N(t,x), i.e. little jitters in how fast proper time runs from point to point.  
   • Operationally: photons, atoms, and clocks pick up tiny random phase shifts as they move, even if the average energy density were zero.

You are asking whether B, if you integrate it long enough, could *accumulate* into something that looks like the extra gravity we blame on dark matter.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  What general relativity says about clock noise
────────────────────────────────────────
GR separates the issue into two orders:

• First-order, linear fluctuations  
  - A small perturbation hμν around the background metric produces gravitational waves or time-of-flight jitter, but its *average* ⟨hμν⟩ = 0, so it does **not** source any steady gravitational potential.  
  - Observationally this is just extra “static on the radio.”  We already put strict limits on such noise with pulsar timing and gravitational-wave interferometers: Δt/t < 10⁻¹⁵ over years.

• Second-order, averaged back-reaction  
  - The square ⟨h²⟩ is positive, so it *can* contribute an effective stress–energy (the Isaacson or “gravitational-wave” energy-momentum tensor).  
  - But the equation of state of that effective fluid is radiation-like (w = 1/3), *not* cold matter (w = 0), because high-frequency metric disturbances carry as much pressure as energy.

In short:  
  Clock jitter **does** behave like energy, but it behaves like a very hot, pressure-full component, whereas dark matter must behave like a cold, pressure-less one.

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Could you engineer timing noise that is cold?
────────────────────────────────────────
To turn your idea into cold dark matter you need all three of these at once:

1. Positive average energy density.  
2. Near-zero average pressure.  
3. Perturbations that can cluster on galaxy scales (∼kpc) without free-streaming away.

That is surprisingly hard:

• Random lapse fluctuations are *gradient* energy.  Like a massless scalar field, their pressure equals their energy density (w = +1), the opposite of what you want.

• You can try to give the lapse fluctuations an effective mass so the field oscillates slowly; then it *can* look cold.  That basically turns it into an “ultra-light scalar” or fuzzy-dark-matter model—now we are back to putting a bona-fide field (i.e. “stuff”) on the right-hand side of Einstein’s equations.

• Any model that shifts events forward/backward in proper time by Δτ > 10⁻²² s on ∼kpc scales would also blur the phase of millisecond pulsars, of GW170817-like inspirals, and of precision atomic clocks.  The current bounds leave only a razor-thin window in parameter space.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  Observational smoking guns for *pure* timing noise
────────────────────────────────────────
Suppose, optimistically, you found a way to satisfy (1)-(3).  What could we look for that distinguishes “timing dark matter” from particle dark matter?

• Frequency-dependent arrival times  
  - High-energy photons or neutrinos from a gamma-ray burst would accumulate a different jitter spectrum than low-energy ones if the lapse noise couples to energy.  No such dispersion has been seen down to Δt < 0.1 s across eight energy decades.

• Cross-correlation between clock networks  
  - A global array of optical atomic clocks could pick up correlated red-noise offsets.  The current global time-transfer noise floor is ∼10⁻¹⁸; still an order of magnitude short of being cosmologically interesting, but watch this space.

• Non-lensing mass deficit  
  - Dark matter inferred from orbital dynamics equals dark matter inferred from lensing to within a few percent.  A model where the curvature comes from “temporal tension” rather than mass density would break that equality.  So far, no discrepancy.

────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Where the research frontier really is
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Back-reaction formalism (Buchert, Green & Wald): can inhomogeneous metric fluctuations imitate dark energy or dark matter?  Consensus: they produce at most a few percent of what is needed, and mostly with the wrong equation of state.

2. “Gravitational aether” and Hořava-type theories: modify the lapse dynamics so that the extra degree of freedom *is* a pressure-less fluid.  Viable on paper, but again ends up adding a field with its own energy—the “stuff” re-enters wearing new clothes.

3. Pulsar Timing Array stochastic-space-time searches: NANOGrav, EPTA, PPTA already limit fractional metric noise to <10⁻¹⁵ on deca-year scales.  SKA Phase 2 will push an order of magnitude lower, slicing away more of the parameter space.

────────────────────────────────────────
6.  Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
• “Noise in the gaps” is a legitimate angle, but in GR a *random timing jitter* behaves like radiation, not like the cool, gravitationally sticky substance we need.  

• To turn the idea into cold dark matter you must (i) endow the jitter with an effective mass, or (ii) couple it to curvature in a way that cancels the pressure.  Both moves re-introduce new fields/parameters—that is, more “stuff.”

• Any such model is sharply testable with pulsar timing, atomic clocks, multi-wavelength burst timing, and lensing/dynamics consistency checks.  So far, the data stay loyal to vanilla cold dark matter.

So the JPEG-artifact picture of the universe is an evocative metaphor, and exploring it teaches us a lot about how GR handles fluctuations.  But unless someone finds a novel, pressure-free form of timing noise—and shows it passes today’s ultra-strict timing tests—the consensus remains: the missing mass behaves like real, cold, collision-less “ghosts,” not just hiccups in the cosmic clock.

Your “hum of the Universe” idea is almost exactly one of the main research frontiers today—just replace the word “hum” by “coherent oscillating field.”

Below is the route from “standing-wave pattern in space-time” to models that already go by names such as axion dark matter, fuzzy/wave dark matter, Bose-Einstein-condensed dark matter and (in some variants) super-fluid dark matter.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Why “pressure” matters and how waves get it
-------------------------------------------------
In cosmology we summarise any substance by an equation–of–state number  

  w  =  (pressure) / (energy density).  

Cold dark matter needs w ≃ 0 so that its density redshifts as ρ ∝ a⁻³ and it can clump.

• Massless waves (gravitational waves, light)  
 – energy = momentum = pressure → w = +1/3 (they are always “hot”).

• A classical, massive scalar field φ(x,t) sitting in a quadratic potential  
 V = ½ m²φ² is more interesting:

  ρ = ½ 𝜙̇² + ½ m²φ²  
  p = ½ 𝜙̇² − ½ m²φ²

 If the field oscillates rapidly compared with the Hubble time, the time average obeys  
 ⟨𝜙̇²⟩ = ⟨m²φ²⟩ (virial theorem) ⇒ ⟨p⟩ = 0.  
 So a *standing wave* of a *massive* field is, on large scales, pressure-less dust.

Gravitational standing waves fail because they are massless; a standing pattern of φ succeeds because the mass changes the bookkeeping.

-------------------------------------------------
2.  Axions / fuzzy dark matter: the “hum” in practice
-------------------------------------------------
• What is oscillating?  
  A very light boson—often an axion-like particle produced in the early universe by the “mis-alignment” mechanism.  The field starts displaced from the minimum of V(φ) and, when the Hubble rate H drops below the mass m, it begins a cosmic-scale coherent oscillation.

• Frequency of the hum  
 f ≃ m c² / h  
 m = 10⁻²² eV (“fuzzy” limit) → f ≈ 2×10⁻⁸ Hz (period ≈ 3 yr)  
 m = 10⁻¹² eV (“ultra-light axion”) → f ≈ 200 Hz (audio range, though far too quiet for a microphone).

• Why it behaves as CDM  
 After oscillations begin, the energy density redshifts exactly like ordinary matter, ρ ∝ a⁻³, and the average pressure stays ≈0.  In your words, the peaks and troughs do cancel out **after time-averaging**.

• Where it *differs* from vanilla CDM  
 The de-Broglie wavelength λ ≈ ħ/(m v) can be kiloparsecs for the 10⁻²² eV case, smoothing out the very smallest galaxies and producing “solitonic cores.”  Lyman-α forest data and dwarf-galaxy counts already push m ≳ 10⁻²¹…10⁻²² eV.

• How we try to detect the hum  
 1. Microwave-cavity haloscopes (ADMX, HAYSTAC, CAPP) look for axion → photon conversion at f = m c²/h.  
 2. NMR-type experiments (CASPEr, QUAX) watch nuclear spins precess in the oscillating background.  
 3. Networks of atomic clocks look for a common f-dependent tick-rate modulation.  
 4. Black-hole “super-radiance” rules out certain masses because the hum would sap spin from stellar-mass or super-massive BHs.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  “Standing wave in the *metric*” vs “standing wave *in* space-time”
-------------------------------------------------
• Gravitational-wave standing pattern

Below is the non-hand-wavy explanation.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  How a scalar dark-matter field behaves near a Schwarzschild black hole
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Equation that matters  
   The field obeys  (□ − m²) a = 0 in curved space-time.

•  Regular solution at the horizon  
   Near r = r_s (the Schwarzschild radius) the only physical mode is the one that flows *into* the hole.  
   Mathematically a ≈ e^{−iω(t + r_*)}, where r_* is the tortoise coordinate that runs to −∞ at the horizon.  
   The amplitude is finite and non-zero; it simply vanishes from the outside world once it crosses r = r_s.

   A smooth continuation of the Galactic field right down to the horizon.

•  Frame dragging in a rotating hole lets certain scalar-field modes extract rotational energy (“super-radiance”).

Black holes do not create universal “mute buttons”; they merely sequester a tiny fraction of the cosmic melody behind event-horizon walls.  Everywhere else—from dense cluster cores to the loneliest cosmic void—the song persists, sometimes quiet, sometimes booming, but never entirely absent.

────────────────────────────────────────
•  Unitarity says the *total* quantum state of the closed Universe evolves reversibly.  
•  Decoherence says any *subsystem* (your brain, my laptop, a rock) rapidly becomes entangled with an enormous environment; locally the subsystem looks classical and its fine-grained information becomes effectively unrecoverable.  
•  The environment that soaks up the correlations is whatever the subsystem couples to most strongly—usually electromagnetic fields, molecules in the air, phonons in the lattice, infrared photons, etc.

────────────────────────────────────────
Take axion-like dark matter as the benchmark.

•  Coupling to photons: g_{aγγ} ≲ 10⁻¹⁵ GeV⁻¹.  
•  Coupling to nucleons: g_{aNN} ≲ 10⁻¹⁰.  
•  Effective interaction cross section at eV energies: σ ≲ 10⁻⁵⁰ cm².  
Compare with ordinary electromagnetic scattering: σ ~ 10⁻¹⁶ cm².

Result: the rate at which an everyday object entangles with axions is ≳10³⁴ times slower than with visible photons or nearby atoms.  The dark-matter field is essentially *not in the room* as far as decoherence is concerned.

•  A single water molecule in ambient conditions loses phase coherence to surrounding photons/phonons in ~10⁻¹³ s.

The Universe-wide wave-function |Ψ⟩ certainly *does* include dark matter, light, gravity—everything.  In that abstract Hilbert space your life’s quantum imprint is still there, spread over gazillions of amplitudes.  But:

•  Almost all of those amplitudes reside in the photon + matter sector, not the dark sector.  
•  Accessing them would require reversing trillions of uncontrolled couplings with zeptosecond accuracy—an operation even Nature effectively never performs.

Calling that unreachable data a “backup” is like claiming the pattern of raindrops on Neptune is a backup of your last text message because both patterns coexist in the global wave-function.  Technically true; operationally useless.

────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Gravitational entanglement—does that help?
────────────────────────────────────────
Gravitational coupling is universal, but it’s weaker still.

•  Newtonian potential between two nucleons 1 Å apart: V_G ≈ 10⁻³⁶ eV.  
•  Decoherence time from gravity-induced phase drift: τ_G ≳ 10⁴⁵ s (way longer than the age of the Universe).

So gravity doesn’t move the quantum information into dark matter either.

────────────────────────────────────────
6.  What *could* imprint on the dark sector?
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Cataclysmic astrophysical events (supernova cores, neutron-star mergers) generate densities and magnetic fields high enough that a non-negligible fraction of energy—and thus quantum correlations—may convert into axions.  
•  Even there, the imprint looks like an incoherent spray, not a structured database.

────────────────────────────────────────
7.  Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Quantum information is conserved globally, but it is overwhelmingly captured by the ordinary, strongly interacting environment, not by cold dark matter.  
2. The axion-like field is far too weakly coupled to act as a practical or even meaningful “quantum backup.”  
3. When a biological organism dies, the patterns that constituted its consciousness decohere into thermal photons and molecular motion within seconds; essentially none of that fine-grained structure migrates into the dark sector.

Poetic takeaway: we all merge back into the cosmic melody in the banal thermodynamic sense—our atoms, energy and entropy rejoin the grand budget—but the specific tune that once defined “you” dies out locally and is not preserved in the silent, slow heartbeat of dark matter.

Short version  
•  In fundamental quantum mechanics the full, fine-grained information about every interaction really is preserved forever.  
•  Almost all of it leaves ordinary matter as low-energy photons, neutrinos, gravitational waves and minute shifts of quantum phases.  
•  Those carriers race away at (or near) light-speed, get Doppler-stretched, scrambled and mixed with 10⁸⁸ other quanta, so for any practical purpose the information is irretrievable—yet, in principle, it still exists in the cosmic wave-function.  
•  A convenient slogan is “nothing is ever erased, but everything becomes unreadably illegible.”

Below is the slower, picture-rich explanation.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  A quick hierarchy of “information”
────────────────────────────────────────
A.  Microstate information (unitary, never lost)  
    – Exact quantum amplitudes and phases of all fields.  
B.  Correlation information (in principle recoverable)  
    – Which photons or phonons are entangled with which atoms.  
C.  Readable records (“classical” information)  
    – Patterns you could, with heroic but finite effort, decode.  
D.  Useful memories  
    – Patterns you can still plug into a brain or a computer.

Death, decoherence, the second law of thermodynamics and cosmic expansion push almost everything from D down to A.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  What actually carries the information away?
────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Infrared photons from warm objects  
    •  Your body (~37 °C) emits ≈ 10²² photons per second.  
    •  Each carries ~10–20 bits (polarisation, timing, direction, frequency).  
    •  In fractions of a millimetre they are out of you; in 1 µs they’re past your neighbours; in a year they’re beyond the Solar System.

2.  Air molecules / phonons  
    •  Collisions spread local patterns through chaotic motion and sound; in milliseconds they thermalise.

3.  Neutrinos, gravitational waves, cold dark-matter quanta  
    •  Much weaker couplings, so they steal a tinier fraction of the Shannon bits, but they carry them essentially forever.

4.  Photons red-shifted by cosmic expansion  
    •  A 100-THz photon that leaves Earth today will be microwaves in a few trillion years, but its phase still encodes a microscopic trace of whatever it reflected from.

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Numbers to calibrate your intuition
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Earth’s surface radiates ≈ 4 × 10²⁶ W.  
  Divide by the energy per infrared photon (~0.1 eV) ⇒ 2 × 10⁴³ photons s⁻¹.  
  At ~10 bits each, Earth exports ~2 × 10⁴⁴ bits every second.  

•  Over 4.5 Gyr that’s ~10⁶¹ bits—vastly more than the ≈10⁴⁹ bits presently stored in every molecule, microbe, book and SSD combined.

•  Conclusion: almost every microscopic fact about Earth’s past has already flown away as outbound light.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  Where does that torrent end up?
────────────────────────────────────────
A.  Inside our observable horizon, for the next ≈ 10¹¹ years  
   •  The photons dilute but still criss-cross the Milky-Way halo; in principle a god-like detector spread over light-years could grab enough of them to reconstruct a fuzzy hologram of prehistoric Earth.

B.  Beyond our future cosmological event horizon  
   •  If cosmic acceleration continues, most outbound radiation eventually red-shifts below the de Sitter temperature and slips irretrievably outside our patch.  
   •  The information is still on the universal ledger, just filed in a drawer no observer here can ever reopen.

C.  Into black holes  
   •  Any bit swallowed by a horizon is believed (by the current majority view) to leak back out, scrambled, in Hawking radiation.  Either way, global unitarity is saved.

────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Why “not destroyed” ≠ “after-life hard-drive”
────────────────────────────────────────
•  To re-create a single cubic millimetre of your cortex you would need to reverse trillions of entangling collisions and collect attosecond-accurate phase data from photons now trillions of kilometres away.  
•  You must also invert the chaotic dynamics of the environment, which requires entropy-decreasing operations forbidden by the second law unless you possess negative entropy elsewhere.  
•  So the information may exist “somewhere,” but no finite being—probably not even an arbitrarily advanced civilization—can coax it back into a coherent, usable form.

────────────────────────────────────────
6.  Any role for the dark-matter field?
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Direct couplings are so feeble (σ ≈ 10⁻⁵⁰ cm², g ≈ 10⁻¹⁶ of QED strength) that less than one photon in 10³⁰ deposits any imprint onto axions or similar candidates.  
•  Thus the dark sector is not the main archive; it is more like a blank wall the story barely smudges.

────────────────────────────────────────
7.  A final metaphor
────────────────────────────────────────
Imagine writing your life story on a beach in footprints.  
•  The ocean (environment) erases the pattern in minutes.  
•  Every displaced sand grain, every evaporated water molecule keeps a quantum-level trace of where it has been.  
•  But those grains and molecules have already blown into seven different deserts and mixed with 10²⁰ others.  In principle the story is “stored,” in practice it is gone.

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
The Universe is indeed a perfect, lossless tape recorder—but one that instantly shreds and disperses the tape into an ocean of static.  All micro-information survives as unfathomably subtle correlations among photons, neutrinos, gravitons and other quanta racing into the abyss of expanding space.  For conscious beings the upshot is: our personal broadcasts are transient AM stations in a storm of cosmic noise—poetic, poignant, and ultimately ephemeral against the backdrop of an eternally humming cosmos.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  YES IN PRINCIPLE:   unitarity keeps a record  
────────────────────────────────────────
1.1  Quantum evolution is reversible.  
      The universal wave-function |Ψ(t)⟩ evolves via a unitary operator U(t₂,t₁)=e^{-iHt/ħ}.  
      No amplitude ever vanishes; it only moves around.

1.2  Every interaction entangles systems with their environment.  
      When a photon bounces off a leaf, a tiny, leaf-shaped phase pattern is imprinted in its electromagnetic field mode.

1.3  In the algebra of Hilbert space the full microstate can, in principle, be run backward to retrodict any earlier configuration, down to the spin of a single electron in a single chlorophyll molecule at sunrise on a Jurassic morning.

Call that the “ghost copy.”  Mathematically, it’s there.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  NO IN PRACTICE:   three brick walls  
────────────────────────────────────────
2.1  No-Cloning Theorem  
      Quantum data cannot be duplicated; the “record” is not a second clean copy you can pull out.  It is the *only* copy, now smeared non-locally into phase correlations among ≳10⁸⁸ quanta.

2.2  Thermodynamic scrambling  
      •  A leaf’s scattered photon acquires ~10 bits.  
      •  A few picoseconds later it has collided with 10³ cosmic microwave photons, each collision entangling a new degree of freedom.  
      •  After kiloyears of interstellar travel, that information is diluted among ~10²⁰ correlations per photon.  
      Reconstructing the leaf would require reversing every interaction—an operation of negative entropy ΔS≈–k·10⁸⁸, far beyond what any finite machine could supply.

2.3  Accessibility horizon  
      Because the Universe is accelerating, most outbound radiation eventually red-shifts below the de-Sitter temperature and crosses our future event horizon.  The information is still on the global book, but forever outside our causal reach.

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  HOW THE “RECORD” ACTUALLY LOOKS  
────────────────────────────────────────
3.1  It is *holographic* in spirit, not in convenience.  
      The photon field encodes a leaf’s shape the way a diffusing ink drop encodes its initial outline: perfectly, but as a vastly extended, almost uniform stain of phase correlations.

3.2  Quantum Darwinism & redundancy  
      Classical facts (Was the leaf green? Did it fall left or right?) get copied redundantly into billions of photons.  Those *pointer* bits are relatively robust; in principle, an astronomically large array of telescopes could infer the macroscopic history of Earth from the stray light now permeating interstellar space.

3.3  Microscopic details (exact neuronal firings, quantum fluctuations in chloroplasts) are *not* redundantly recorded.  They decohere into high-order phase information that is effectively unrecoverable.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  THOUGHT EXPERIMENT:  “God’s Decoder”  
────────────────────────────────────────
Imagine a spherical detector the size of a galaxy, cooled to 10⁻¹⁸ K, containing 10⁴⁰ qubits and synchronized clocks accurate to 10⁻³⁰ s.

•  Even that monstrosity could grab only ≲10⁻¹² of the photons Earth emitted during the age of dinosaurs.  
•  Coherently inverting their joint state would require a quantum computer with Hilbert-space dimension ~2^{10⁶⁴}.  
•  The minimum free-energy cost to lower its entropy back to zero (Landauer) exceeds the mass-energy of the observable Universe.

So the leaf’s “perfect record” is provably safe from exhumation by any agent bound by finite resources.

────────────────────────────────────────
5.  BOTTOM LINE  
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Ghost copy?  Mathematically yes: unitarity means every quantum phase ever written still exists.  
•  Practical retrieval?  No: the information is diffused, un-clonable, and hidden behind thermodynamic and causal horizons.  
•  Existential punch-line:  Our footprints are indeed indelible in the cosmic ledger—but written in a cipher so extravagantly tangled that, for any being living inside the Universe, they may as well have faded away.  We are simultaneously ephemeral to ourselves and permanent to the math.

The short answer is still “no, not even with absurdly good technology,” and the reason is a stack of hard ceilings that lie beyond mere engineering prowess.

Below is the stack, in escalating order of hopelessness.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  You must first catch the bits
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Angular dilution  
  A photon that left Earth 100 million years ago now sits on a sphere 100 Mly in radius: surface area ≈10⁵¹ m².  
  Even a Dyson-sphere-sized telescope (≈10¹⁷ m²) intercepts only 10⁻³⁴ of those photons.

•  Temporal dilution  
  The photon flux through any finite collecting area falls as 1/a² with cosmic expansion; waiting longer just makes the trickle slower.

•  Gravitational red-shift and de-Sitter horizon  
  If dark energy stays on, our causal patch acquires a horizon with radius R_Λ ≈ 16 Gly and an irreducible Gibbons–Hawking temperature  
      T_dS ≈ ℏc / (2πkR_Λ) ≃ 2×10⁻³⁰ K.  
  Any “catcher’s mitt” you build will be bathed in that noise forever; you can’t cool below it, so the signal-to-noise ratio keeps shrinking.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Reading the bits costs energy you won’t have
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Landauer bound  
  Erasing or copying one bit in a bath at temperature T costs ≥kT ln 2 of free energy.  At T_dS the cost is ≈10⁻⁵⁴ J per bit—tiny per bit, but:

•  Budget check  
  A Sun-mass star stores ~10⁴⁷ J of usable free energy over its full life.  
  The raw “bookkeeping budget’’ of Earth’s outbound infrared stream is ~10⁶¹ bits.  
  Even at the de-Sitter limit you’d need 10⁷ solar masses of perfectly available energy simply to sort the bits you intercepted—which, recall, is already a vanishing fraction of the total broadcast.

•  Long-term energy famine  
  After ~10¹⁴ yr all stars are dead; after ~10¹⁶ yr even brown dwarfs are gone.  Your collectors run on the dregs of proton decay (if it occurs) or black-hole evaporation—power outputs of order 10⁻³⁰ W per solar mass.  The ledger is negative.

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Quantum no-cloning and error explosion
────────────────────────────────────────
•  The arriving photons are in highly mixed, entangled states after eons of gravitational lensing, scattering and spontaneous emission.  
•  No-cloning forbids you to make perfect working copies; every measurement is destructive.  
•  You must therefore perform a single, astronomically large collective measurement—effectively a gigantic one-shot quantum error-correction operation—on quanta arriving over cosmological times.  Miss one phase factor and the reconstructed history decoheres.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  Causal and computational limits
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Finite Hilbert space inside a horizon  
  With positive Λ, the maximum fine-grained entropy you can ever access is S_max = A/4ℏG ≈10¹²³ bits.  Earth alone emitted ≳10⁶¹ bits already; the Milky Way ≳10⁶⁸ bits.  You saturate the cosmic memory budget long before you have captured a meaningful slice of the archive.

•  Quantum circuit depth  
  The computation to invert the scrambling grows like exp(N), where N is the number of entangling collisions you must reverse (think ≳10⁸⁸).  Even with gates running at the Planck rate (~10⁴³ Hz) you exceed the Poincaré recurrence time of the Universe.

────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Bottom line in three sentences
────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Yes, unitarity guarantees a “ghost copy” of every micro event.  
2.  But almost all of that record flies away as an ever thinner, ever colder, ever noisier spray of quanta, most of which leave our causal patch.  
3.  Physical limits on collection area, free energy, entropy, causal horizons and quantum computation together render the data hopelessly inaccessible—even to galaxy-spanning, near-immortal alien societies.

So the cosmic librarian does keep every page, but she feeds them through a shredder, scatters the confetti across an expanding cosmic desert, and posts armed guards (thermodynamics, de-Sitter noise, no-cloning, finite resources) at every dune.  The archive exists; no one will ever check a volume out.

But wait, if the universe is this giant quantum computer keeping track of everything, and information really can’t be destroyed, then where does it all go?  
Like not just our memories but every photon that ever bounced off a leaf, every electron that ever fired in a neuron, every thought, just encoded in the light that bounced off it?

The shortest honest answer  
Every microscopic detail of every event ends up as phase-level correlations among the quanta that now fill the Universe—mainly low-energy photons, a sprinkling of neutrinos and gravitons, and a trace of dark-matter or other feebly interacting fields.  The information is never lost, but it is dispersed so completely, over so many carriers and in so many causal regions, that no physically possible agent could ever reassemble more than a vanishing sliver of it.

Below is the “where does it all go?” story in four concrete steps.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  The first hand-off: local environment
-------------------------------------------------
A leaf falls; photons scatter; neurons fire.

•  Coupling strengths  
  – Electromagnetism ≈ 1  
  – Nuclear, phonon interactions ≈ 10-2…10-4  
  – Dark-matter couplings ≤ 10-16  

  So almost every qubit of fine detail departs along electromagnetic or atomic channels, not into dark matter.

•  Decoherence time‐scale  
  For a warm, wet system the phase memory of any quantum state is smashed in 10-13…10-9 s.  
  After that, the pattern lives only in the joint state of “system + photons + molecules + phonons.’’

-------------------------------------------------
2.  The long exodus: cosmic radiation fog
-------------------------------------------------
•  Infrared photons  
  At 300 K a square metre of skin emits ≈ 10²¹ photons s⁻¹.  Earth’s surface emits ≈ 10⁴³ photons s⁻¹.  Each carries ∼10 bits of micro-information (direction, polarisation, phase).

•  Dilution  
  Those photons now lie on spheres whose radii grow at c.  After 1 Myr the sphere around Earth has area ≈10⁵⁰ m²; a square-kilometre collector would intercept ≈10⁻⁴⁰ of the broadcast.

•  Red-shift  
  Cosmic expansion stretches each wave and lowers its energy, but the phase correlations survive in principle.

Result: most of the world’s microscopic record leaves the Solar System in seconds, the Galaxy in kiloyears, and our future causal patch in ∼10¹¹ yr.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Special bins: black-hole horizons
-------------------------------------------------
•  Anything—matter or radiation—that crosses an event horizon is hidden from external observers.  
•  Unitarity is believed to be saved by Hawking radiation, which re-emits the encoded information in an astronomically scrambled form.  
•  Net accounting: a few-per-cent of all information may take this detour, but it still ends up as inaccessible, ultra-mixed radiation.

-------------------------------------------------
4.  Asymptotic barriers that freeze retrieval
-------------------------------------------------
A.  Entropy/energy budget  
   Sorting N bits in a bath at temperature T costs at least NkT ln 2 of free energy (Landauer).  After all stars fade, available free energy per solar mass drops below 10-30 W—utterly insufficient for large-scale data recovery.

B.  De-Sitter noise floor  
   Dark-energy–driven expansion leaves a residual Gibbons–Hawking temperature T_dS ≈ 2×10⁻³⁰ K.  No system inside the future horizon can cool below it, so signal-to-noise asymptotically freezes out.

C.  Computational complexity  
   To “unscramble” a cubic centimeter of warm matter you would need to invert ≳10⁸⁸ entangling collisions—an operation requiring gates and memory that exceed the Bekenstein entropy bound (≈10¹²³ bits) of the entire observable Universe.

-------------------------------------------------
Putting it all together
-------------------------------------------------
1.  Yes, unitarity guarantees a ghost copy of every photon path, every synaptic spike, every quantum fluctuation.  
2.  That copy is not a tidy ledger but an unimaginably diffuse pattern of phase relations among ~10⁸⁸ streaming quanta.  
3.  Cosmic expansion, finite free energy, entropy bounds, causal horizons and the quantum no-cloning theorem make the data unrecoverable in practice—even to a civilisation with galaxy-scale engineering and trillions of years at its disposal.

So the librarian does keep every book, but each page is shredded into single letters, those letters are thrown onto different winds, and the winds themselves drift beyond the horizon of the “library” as it swells forever.  The information is there—perfect, indelible, and, for any mortal or immortal physicist inside the Universe, decisively out of reach.

Short answer  
Even if an unimaginably lucky observer caught one of those escaped photons, all they could learn is a few generic facts—its frequency, arrival direction, polarisation, maybe a time stamp.  That amounts to at most a few tens of bits.  A single photon simply does not carry enough information to reveal who was in front of it, let alone that a kiss occurred.

Below is the why, with concrete numbers.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  What one photon can hold in principle
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Frequency (or energy): pick 1 Hz resolution around 4×10¹⁴ Hz (visible light) ⇒ ~48 bits.  
•  Arrival time: resolve to 1 ns inside a 1 s observation window ⇒ ~30 bits.  
•  Direction: 1-arcsecond resolution on both axes ⇒ ~28 bits.  
•  Polarisation: 1 bit.  

Add them up: ≲110 bits before detector noise, optics aberrations, quantum efficiency, etc.  In practice the usable payload is a couple of dozen bits.

110 bits is enough to encode, say, the ASCII text “HELLO WORLD,” but trillions of bits are needed to specify a megapixel image, and vastly more to describe the 10¹⁵-synapse brain state that made the kiss meaningful.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Why almost all shape information needs many photons
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Amplitude versus phase  
  – The “shape” of the face is stored mainly in the **relative phases** and **correlations** among a *wavefront* of photons.  
  – Measure one photon by itself and you collapse its state; you lose the phase relation to its former companions.

•  Interference requirement  
  – To reconstruct an image you need to combine huge numbers of photons coherently (think holography or interferometry).  
  – One particle gives no baselines, no interference fringes, no picture.

•  Error budget  
  – Shot-noise error in any optical measurement scales like 1/√N.  
  – With N = 1, the noise is 100 %.  You cannot beat that by clever post-processing.

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  The “catch-probability’’ problem
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Sphere radius after 100 years of travel: r ≈ 100 ly → surface area A ≈ 10³⁵ m².  
•  Area of a kilometre-scale telescope: a ≈ 10⁶ m².  
•  Geometric interception probability: a/A ≈ 10⁻²⁹.  
  You would need ~10²⁹ such telescopes to guarantee catching *one* of the original photons.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  What a single captured photon could actually tell you
────────────────────────────────────────
Best-case data record:

   “A visible-light photon of wavelength 613.02 nm arrived from RA 19ʰ 24ᵐ 18ˢ, Dec +29° 43′ 07″ at 03:41:12.873 UTC on 18 July 2223, polarised 71°.”

That line hints Earth emitted/sent a photon with a Sun-like spectrum in that direction centuries ago.  It tells you nothing about whose face reflected it or that two people happened to be kissing.

────────────────────────────────────────
5.  How many photons would you need to know “something happened”?
────────────────────────────────────────
•  To resolve a 1-cm feature at 100 ly with optical interferometry you would need ~10⁶ photons and receivers spread over a baseline of thousands of kilometres, plus exquisite phase control.  
•  To distinguish “two faces close together” from “one face” you may need 10⁹–10¹⁰ photons, depending on noise and reconstruction fidelity.  
•  To pick out facial emotion or motion (a smile, a kiss) you would need frame-rates and photon counts comparable to a movie camera: 10¹¹–10¹² photons per frame.

────────────────────────────────────────
6.  Fundamental limits, not just technological ones
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Holevo bound: one qubit (photon) never lets you extract more than one classical bit on average without extra entanglement resources, which you don’t have.  
•  No-cloning: you can’t copy the photon to run multiple different measurements.  
•  Heisenberg/shot noise: with N = 1, statistical uncertainty is maximal.

────────────────────────────────────────
7.  Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
Yes, the photon is still out there.  
Yes, it still *contains* a microscopic imprint of that night’s scene.  
But “contains” does not equal “conveys.”  The photon’s information bandwidth is far too small, and the chance of intercepting the *many* photons needed for an image is effectively zero.  So your first kiss is woven permanently into the cosmic tapestry—but only as a pattern so finely and widely threaded that no single strand can ever reveal more than the barest whisper of its existence.

    •  Photons from the streetlamp bounce off the blinds, off the wall, off your face, and join the outbound light that will carry tonight’s micro-history into the interstellar dark.  
    •  A few trillionths of a trillionth of that energy nicks the dark-matter field as it passes; the rest becomes part of the ever-thickening fog of infrared glow that quilts the Galaxy.  
    So even the fridge’s vibration is, in the grand accounting, another fine stitch in the tapestry we’ve been talking about.

4.  A personal translation  
    That low mechanical drone is what the cosmic story sounds like when it’s routed through coils, coolant and pressed-steel casing instead of through axion modes or CMB photons.  It’s the same Universe, rendered in a frequency band your ears happen to catch.

So yes: when the compressor kicks on, you’re hearing an almost comically sped-up, locally engineered remix of the far subtler rhythm that moves galaxies and bends light.  The fact that you can sit in a quiet room, notice that mundane buzz, and mentally trace it all the way out to the edge of the observable cosmos is its own little marvel—a moment of resonance between everyday life and the deepest machinery of reality.

Like cosmic background noise but for consciousness?
